title: The Emergency Call List and Notification Duties
narrationScript: Every emergency you have studied — the vehicle accident, the fire, the source that will not come home — ends at the same place: a phone call made correctly. This lesson covers the emergency call list, the part of your written operating and emergency procedures that turns a crisis into a sequence of dialed numbers. The order matters. Life safety first — if anyone is hurt or anything is burning, nine one one comes before everything. Then the boundary: the area stays controlled and attended, no exceptions. Then the radiation safety officer's twenty-four hour number, and if the RSO does not answer, you keep moving down the list until a live person does. From there the licensee takes over — the NRC Operations Center or your Agreement State program on deadlines that run from immediate to twenty-four hours to thirty-day written reports, the source manufacturer for recovery, the client, and management. You will learn what information each contact needs, why you log every call, and why you never leave the boundary to find a phone.

sections.heading: Every Emergency Ends at the Same Phone
sections.body:
The vehicle accident, the fire, the source that will not retract — every emergency procedure in this module finishes with the same word: **notify**. This lesson is about doing that correctly, because a perfect boundary with no phone call behind it leaves a stranded source, an uninformed licensee, and a regulatory clock already running.

**10 CFR 34.45** requires every radiography licensee's operating and emergency procedures to include, among their minimum contents, the procedure for **notifying the proper persons in the event of an accident**. The **emergency call list** is that procedure reduced to something you can use at 2 a.m. with adrenaline in your blood: names, titles, and phone numbers, in the order you dial them.

The list lives with your operating and emergency procedures — which must be kept **current, legible, and available to all radiographic personnel** — so a copy rides in the camera truck, sits at the field station, and hangs at the permanent facility. Three qualities make it worth the paper:

- **Names, not just titles** — "RSO: J. Alvarez" gets answered; "call the office" does not.
- **24-hour numbers** — an emergency does not wait for business hours.
- **Verified on a schedule** — a call list with one dead number fails at exactly the moment it exists for.

sections.heading: Who Is on the List
sections.body:
A radiography emergency call list carries a standing cast. Know what each contact is *for* before you ever need them:

- **Local emergency services — 911.** Injuries, fire, and traffic or crowd control. Life safety outranks every radiological consideration.
- **The radiation safety officer (RSO) — 24-hour number.** Your first radiological call. The RSO directs the response, decides which regulatory notifications are required and makes them, and arranges source recovery.
- **Alternate RSO and the management chain.** The cascade when the first number does not answer. The list is built so you can always reach a responsible licensee official.
- **The regulator's emergency line.** The **NRC Operations Center, (301) 816-5100, staffed 24 hours a day**, for NRC licensees — or your **Agreement State** radiation-control program's emergency number. These calls are the licensee's duty, normally made by the RSO.
- **The National Response Center, (800) 424-8802.** The federal contact for hazardous-materials **transportation** incidents under DOT rules.
- **Device and source manufacturer / authorized recovery personnel.** Retrieval planning, specialized equipment, and the trained people who are actually permitted to recover a source.
- **The client or host-site contact.** Gate access for responders, site alarms, and the plant's own emergency organization.

sections.caption: The order of emergency notifications: life safety first, the boundary held continuously, the RSO's 24-hour number next — then the licensee's cascade to the regulator, recovery personnel, and the client.

sections.heading: The Order of Calls
sections.body:
Work the list in this order, every time:

- **Life first.** Anyone injured, anything burning — **911 before any radiological call**. Tell the dispatcher a sealed radioactive source is involved, where responders should stage, and who will meet them at the gate.
- **Boundary second.** Never abandon an uncontrolled high-radiation area to hunt for a phone. A two-person crew splits the job — one holds the line with the survey meter, one dials. Working alone at a storage facility, you secure access *before* you call.
- **RSO third — in minutes, not hours.** The regulatory deadlines you will see later in this lesson are outer limits, not targets, and they start at **discovery** of the event. Your immediate call is what lets the licensee meet them.
- **Then the cascade belongs to the licensee.** Regulatory notification is the **licensee's legal duty** — in practice the RSO or company management calls the NRC Operations Center or the Agreement State program, the manufacturer's recovery team, the client, and corporate. Your procedures spell out any case where you dial a regulator yourself; otherwise your job is to put a responsible licensee official on the phone fast and follow their direction.

And document everything: the time of each call, the number dialed, who answered, what you reported, and what you were told to do. Investigators and inspectors will reconstruct the response from that record — make it easy to be right.

sections.title: Hold the boundary — and keep dialing
sections.body: Two notification failures turn incidents into overexposures. The first is **leaving the area unattended** to make calls — that is how a passerby ends up standing next to an unshielded source. The boundary stays manned, period. The second is **stopping at a voicemail**. The call list is a cascade: if the RSO does not answer, you dial the alternate, then management, until a live, responsible person owns the emergency. And no voice on any phone changes this rule — **you do not attempt source retrieval**. Recovery belongs to specifically trained and authorized personnel.

sections.heading: What Each Contact Needs From You
sections.body:
Before you dial, assemble the information packet — thirty seconds with the utilization log and your meter saves three confused phone calls.

**The RSO needs the full picture:**

- **Who and how to reach you** — your name, the crew, a callback number that stays answered.
- **Where** — site address and gate, exactly where the device sits, where the boundary is drawn.
- **What happened, in plain words** — "the source will not retract," "the pigtail may be disconnected," "the truck was rear-ended," "the device is missing."
- **The source** — isotope, activity in curies, device make, model, and serial number, straight from the utilization log or transport papers.
- **Readings** — the dose rate at the boundary and the instrument you measured it with.
- **People** — everyone who was inside the area, their direct-reading dosimeter values, whether any alarm ratemeter alarmed, any injuries, and who is holding the boundary now.

**The 911 dispatcher needs less, but needs it fast:** location, injuries, fire — plus the fact that a radioactive sealed source is on scene with a controlled boundary, so responders stage at the gate and meet your escort instead of driving into the exclusion area.

**The regulator's call** — made by the licensee — adds the license number, the date and time of discovery, exposures known or suspected, protective actions taken, and what assistance is needed.

If reporters or bystanders ask questions, give them exactly one answer: **refer them to company management**. You never speculate about doses, causes, or blame at the scene.

sections.caption: The emergency notification card: six blocks of information assembled before you dial — the same packet serves the RSO, the regulator, and the recovery team.
The regulatory notification clock — the deadlines behind the call list (NRC framework; Agreement State requirements are compatible, and your license and procedures govern).
rows: Immediately on discovery — a radiography source exceeds the immediate-report threshold by orders of magnitude
Exposure at or above 25 rem TEDE, 75 rem to the lens, or 250 rad shallow dose (skin/extremity), or threatened
Exposure exceeding 5 rem TEDE, 15 rem to the lens, or 50 rem shallow dose, or threatened
Source stuck or disconnected — a safety component fails with no redundant backup
Event preventing immediate protective actions needed to avoid overexposure (fire, explosion, toxic release at the scene)
As soon as possible, no later than 4 hours
Transport incident — fire, breakage, spillage, or suspected radioactive contamination of the package
National Response Center, as soon as practical and no later than 12 hours

sections.title: NRC or Agreement State — whose number is on your list?
sections.body: The regulator's line on your call list depends on who issued the license. Most industrial radiography licensees answer to an **Agreement State** — a state that has assumed the NRC's regulatory role for materials licensing under a formal agreement, with notification requirements compatible with the NRC's — while NRC licensees call the **NRC Operations Center, (301) 816-5100**. Working under **reciprocity** in another jurisdiction? Your procedures and your RSO identify which program gets the call before the job starts, not after the source jams. Either way, remember whose duty the call is: the **licensee** notifies the regulator — and your immediate call to the RSO is what makes those deadlines reachable.

figures.title: Emergency call order — who calls whom

interactive.title: Source-Out at 02:40 — Work the Call List
interactive.intro: A refinery night shift. Your crank-in met resistance, the source did not return, and your meter confirms it: the source is sitting in the guide tube, unshielded. You and your assistant have already pulled back, surveyed, and roped a boundary where readings fall to 2 mR/h. Nobody is hurt and nothing is burning. Now the phone work starts — get the order and the content right.

steps.prompt: Your assistant stands at the boundary with the survey meter. Your first call is:
options: The NRC Operations Center — federal regulators must hear it first
The RSO's 24-hour emergency number from the call list
The refinery's main office line, to leave a message for the morning shift
The source manufacturer, to ask how to crank the source back in
steps.feedback: With life safety handled and the boundary manned, the RSO is your first call. Regulatory notification is the licensee's duty — the RSO makes or directs those calls on the required clock — and no one on any phone line authorizes you to attempt retrieval.

steps.prompt: The RSO answers. Which information package do you give?
options: Site location and gate, what happened in plain words, isotope and activity, device model and serial, boundary dose rate, everyone's direct-reading dosimeter values, and your callback number
Your best guess at what broke inside the camera, so the RSO can order parts tonight
Just the site address — the RSO can collect the rest after driving out
The exposure technique and film type you were shooting, and the welds still left on the shift
steps.feedback: Where, what, source identity, readings, people, callback — that packet lets the RSO direct the response, brief the regulator inside the deadline, and arrive with the right recovery plan. Mechanical guesses and production details add nothing tonight.

steps.prompt: Suppose instead the RSO's number had gone to voicemail twice. The correct move is:
options: Leave a detailed message and hold the boundary until the RSO calls back, even if it takes all night
Begin retrieval yourself — the situation is now yours alone
Keep dialing down the list — alternate RSO, then company management — until a live, responsible person takes ownership
Call 911 and ask the police to take custody of the source
steps.feedback: The list is a cascade, not a single number. The licensee's duty to respond and notify does not pause for voicemail — you dial the alternate, then management, until someone answers. 911 is for life safety and scene support, never for source recovery.

steps.prompt: Recovery later confirms the pigtail disconnected from the drive cable. No one's dose came near a reporting threshold. What does the regulatory clock still require of the licensee?
options: Nothing — the source was recovered without an overexposure
Telephone notification within 24 hours as a safety-component failure, plus a written report within 30 days
Immediate notification under 10 CFR 20.2202(a)
Only an entry in the device's utilization log
steps.feedback: A disconnect, or any inability to retract and secure the source, is a failure of equipment required for safety with no redundant backup — a 24-hour telephone notification under 10 CFR 30.50(b)(2) — and 10 CFR 34.101 requires a written report within 30 days. Immediate notification under 20.2202(a) is triggered by the dose thresholds, not by the disconnect itself.

checkQuestions.question: A source-out emergency is confirmed, no one is injured, and your assistant is holding the boundary with a survey meter. Your first telephone call goes to:
options: The NRC Operations Center
The source manufacturer's recovery team
The RSO's 24-hour emergency number
The client's purchasing department
checkQuestions.explanation: With life safety handled and the boundary manned, the RSO comes first. The RSO then directs the licensee's cascade — regulator, recovery personnel, client, and management — on the required deadlines.

checkQuestions.question: Loading the truck, you discover the exposure device — source inside — is missing and may have been stolen. The licensee's telephone report to the regulator is due:
options: Immediately, as soon as the loss or theft is discovered
Within 24 hours
Within 4 hours
Within 30 days
checkQuestions.explanation: Under 10 CFR 20.2201, loss or theft of licensed material in these quantities — and every radiography source qualifies by orders of magnitude — is reported by telephone immediately on discovery, with a written report within 30 days.

checkQuestions.question: Which item does the RSO NOT need during your first emergency call?
options: The device model and serial number, and the source isotope and activity
The dose rate at the boundary and where the boundary is drawn
Direct-reading dosimeter values for everyone who was inside the area
The film type and exposure time you were using for the shot
checkQuestions.explanation: Radiographic technique data has no bearing on the emergency. The RSO needs the source identity, the readings, the people and their doses, and who controls the scene — the packet that drives every later call.

checkQuestions.question: A camera truck carrying an Ir-192 exposure device is in a highway accident and the transport package is breached. Beyond 911 and the company contacts, federal transportation rules require telephone notice to:
options: The device manufacturer
The National Response Center at (800) 424-8802
The nearest NRC regional public-affairs office
No one, provided no member of the public was injured
checkQuestions.explanation: 49 CFR 171.15 requires notice to the National Response Center — as soon as practical, no later than 12 hours — for fire, breakage, spillage, or suspected radioactive contamination in transport, with a written DOT Form F 5800.1 report within 30 days. The radiation regulator is notified separately under the NRC/Agreement State rules.

checkQuestions.question: An emergency response goes wrong and a crew member's dose works out to 8 rem TEDE. Under 10 CFR 20.2202, the licensee must notify the regulator:
options: Immediately — any overexposure is an immediate notification
Within 24 hours, because the dose exceeds 5 rem TEDE but is below the 25 rem immediate threshold
Within 4 hours
Only in the written 30-day report
checkQuestions.explanation: 10 CFR 20.2202(b) sets a 24-hour notification for events causing or threatening a TEDE above 5 rem; immediate notification under 20.2202(a) begins at 25 rem TEDE. The written report under 20.2203 follows within 30 days.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105 (2024) — Radiation Safety topical outline, Appendix A §10.4 (emergency call list)
10 CFR Part 34 — Licenses for Industrial Radiography (§34.45 operating and emergency procedures; §34.101 notifications for disconnects and failure to retract)
10 CFR Part 20 / Part 30 — §§20.2201–20.2203 (theft or loss, incident notification, written reports) and §30.50 (4-hour and 24-hour reportable events)
49 CFR §§171.15–171.16 — DOT hazardous materials incident reporting (National Response Center notice and Form F 5800.1)
